coredata.dc:title: Long-term antibacterial composite via alginate aerogel sustained release of antibiotics and Cu used for bone tissue bacteria infection

Zhang, Yu
Hu, Tian
Li, Wei-Xiong
Li, Zeng-Lin
Hu, Han-Jian
Zhu, Shui-Rong
Chen, Wen-Zhao
Zhou, Chu-Song
Jiang, Gang-Biao
coredata.dc:description: Bone related-bacterial diseases including wound infections and osteomyelitis (OM) remain a serious problem accompanied with amputation in most severe cases. In this work, we report an exceptional effective antibacterial alginate aerogel, which consists of tigecycline (TGC) and octahedral Cu crystal as an organo-inorganic synergy platform for antibacterial and local infection therapy applications. The alginate aerogel could greatly prolong the release of copper ions and maintain effective antibacterial concentration over 18 days. The result of in-vitro experiments demonstrated that the alginate aerogel has an exceptional effective function on antibacterial activity. Cytotoxicity tests indicated that the alginate aerogel has low biological toxicity (average cell viability >75%). These remarkable results suggested that the alginate aerogel exhibits great potential for the treatment of OM, and has a prosperous future of application in bone tissue engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural polymers
Osteomyelitis
Long-term antibacterial effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of drug-loading and entrapment efficiency
Evaluation of swelling properties and retention properties
In vitro drug release studies and ion release measurements
In vitro drug release from Cu/TGC@PDA
In vitro antimicrobial activity of Cu/TGC@PDA for bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Cu/TGC@PDA. (a) SEM image of Cu crystal after 20 min. (b) SEM image of Cu crystal after 24 h. (c) SEM image of Cu/TGC@PDA. (d) Size distribution of Cu, TGC, Cu/TGC and Cu/TGC@PDA. (e) FTIR patterns for Cu,TGC,Cu/TGC and Cu/TGC@PDA. (f) XRD for Cu.
Structural characterization of aerogel. (a) SEM image of SA-Gel aerogel. (b) SEM image of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel. (c) FT-IR patterns for SA, Gel, OSA and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel.

Structural characterization of aerogel. (a) Rate of water absorption for Cu aerogel, Cu/TGC aerogel, Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel and SA-Gel aerogel. (b) Photos of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel in culture dishes before and after swelling under day light.
(a) Cumulative release curves of TGC from TGC aerogel, Cu/TGC aerogel and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel in PBS (PH = 7.4) at 37 °C. (b) Cu2+ released from Cu aerogel, Cu@PDA aerogel and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel.
Cytotoxicity of five different concentration's materials treated with mouse fibroblast cells treated for 24 h (n = 5, *P < 0.05).

The effect of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel on hUC-MSCs viability. (a) Fluorescence microscopic images of the hU-MSCs cultured for 1, 3 and 5 days. (b) CCK-8 assay of hU-MSCs on Cu/TGC@PDA and SA-Gel aerogel cultured for 1, 3 and 5 days (n = 3, *p < 0.05).

In vitro evaluation of antibacterial activity of different aerogel. (a) Diameter datum of zone of inhibition of TGC, Cu, Cu/TGC and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel against E. coli. (b) Diameter datum of zone of inhibition of TGC, Cu, Cu/TGC and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel against S. aureus. (c) Antibacterial activity of TGC(A), Cu(B), Cu/TGC(C) and Cu/TGC@PDA(D) aerogel (after 24 h). (d) Antibacterial activity of different concentrations of Cu/TGC@PDA (A: 8 mg/mL; B: 6 mg/mL; C: 4 mg/mL; D: 2 mg/mL; E: positive control).
Schematic illustration of the fabrication of Cu/TGC@PDA alginate aerogel.
Zeta potential and hydrodynamic size of Cu, TGC, Cu/TGC, Cu/TGC@PDA.
Drug loading and entrapment efficiency of Cu/TGC.

head.ce:title: Long-term antibacterial composite via alginate aerogel sustained release of antibiotics and Cu used for bone tissue bacteria infection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bone related-bacterial diseases including wound infections and osteomyelitis (OM) remain a serious problem accompanied with amputation in most severe cases. In this work, we report an exceptional effective antibacterial alginate aerogel, which consists of tigecycline (TGC) and octahedral Cu crystal as an organo-inorganic synergy platform for antibacterial and local infection therapy applications. The alginate aerogel could greatly prolong the release of copper ions and maintain effective antibacterial concentration over 18 days. The result of in-vitro experiments demonstrated that the alginate aerogel has an exceptional effective function on antibacterial activity. Cytotoxicity tests indicated that the alginate aerogel has low biological toxicity (average cell viability >75%). These remarkable results suggested that the alginate aerogel exhibits great potential for the treatment of OM, and has a prosperous future of application in bone tissue engineering.

ce:section.ce:para: Osteomyelitis is an infection bone disease often caused by bacterial, which is one of the oldest diseases in history [1]. A bone infection caused the medullary and the periosteal blood supplies in hindered, leading to the formation of sequestra or dead bone [2]. Patients with osteomyelitis cause fever, local pain, hyposthenia and trauma, in severe cases, leading to amputation [3]. The common local drug delivery for the treatment of osteomyelitis is polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) loaded with gentamycin or tobramycin, and offered in cement form or in bead form [4,5]. However, some studies have reported that PMMA has many drawbacks including (1) non biodegradability that necessitates revision surgery [6], (2) the exothermic reaction which decreases the effectiveness of certain antibiotics [7], and (3) lack of flexibility. To circumvent these issues, a series of biodegradable bio-materials loaded with antibiotics, such as poly-(lactic acid) [8], calcium phosphate cement [9] and various other polymers have been reported in recent years [10–13]. Unfortunately, osteomyelitis often infected by varieties of bacteria, resulting in uneffective inhibition if just using a single antibiotic. Moreover, osteomyelitis debridement surgery always accompanies with residual bacteria in the bone tissue, as a consequence in a high recurrence rate of osteomyelitis [14]. Therefore, an ideal anti-osteomyelitis material should have a continuous long-term antibacterial effect and excellent physical and mechanical properties.

Aerogel is an exceptional group of nanoporous materials with open pore structure of high surface area and considered as promising carriers for drug delivery [15] and tissue engineering scaffold [16,17]. Sodium alginate (SA), the sodium salt of alginic acid, has unique features including biocompatibility, controlled release properties, and biodegradability [18]. Recently, SA-based aerogel has gained considerable attention due to the high surface area [19], low density [20], abundant porosity and good mechanical properties [21]. Raman et al. reported a silver loaded calcium-alginate aerogel, which possesses excellent antibacterial ability [22]. In García et al. study, antibacterial sodium alginate dry aerogel with ketoprofen and benzoic acid was fabricated, which could maintain effective antibacterial concentration over 120 min [23]. However, the increasing resistance of the pathogenic bacteria limits antibiotic effectiveness, which prolongs the therapy process and resulting that a single strategy is far from sufficient for bone tissue bacteria infections. Recent studies reported that some bacteria resistance was weakened in the presence of metal ions, mainly because the metals ions inhibit the growth of pathogenic microorganisms even at low concentration [24–27]. In addition, the combination of metal nanoparticles with antibiotics could engender exceptional effective effect on killing multi-drug resistant bacterial strains have been confirmed in reported literature [28]. Hence, new low-cost material integrated of metals and antibiotics will be a promising therapeutic tool in this field.

Taking the effective antibacterial of copper into consideration, the copper-based materials have attracted burgeoning attention in recent years due to the properties of low-cost and long term antibacterial activity [29]. It has been demonstrated that copper ions (Cu2+) involved many wound-healing-related processes, including favoring the development of new vessels [30,31], restoring the normal rate of bone resorption [32] and enhancing collagen fiber deposition [33]. Nevertheless, copper-based therapy suffers from restriction, because the copper salt could rapidly be converted into an ionic state with high concentration [34] and lead to pathological changes such as liver and kidney tissue damage [35], and even to death. Some studies demonstrated that the toxicity of copper can be reduced if the copper ion release rate was slowed down [36,37].

Several studies have demonstrated that carbon dots (CDs) can act as a stabilizer for blocking the aggregation of metal nanoparticles [38] and provide multiple active sites [39,40]. Carbon material can also slow down the release of metal ions by coating on the surface of the metal and delaying its corrosion process [41]. Thus it is promising to obtain Cu inorganic complexes that could slowly-release Cu2+ in the long term. Herein, we synthesized a octahedral Cu crystal based on our previously reported work [42], the CDs induced to the self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles through organics-inorganics interface interaction, and formed self-assemblies mesocrystal with tunable morphology. The carbon dots as the first slow-release layer immobilized on the surface of Cu crystal and bounded with antibiotics. A well knows antibiotics, tigecycline (TGC), were chosen as a model drug for its broad-spectrum antibacterial properties on killing the most bacteria. Because of the high lattice-matching of the Cu crystal with CDs [43], this copper‑carbon dots complex could form an octahedral structure that controlled the Cu2+ released rate and produced a long-term antibacterial effect, which was beneficial to reduce the recurrence rate of osteomyelitis. Polydopamine (PDA) acted as a second slow-release layer wrapt around the complex and improved the water solubility [44]. Simultaneously, the combination of the copper ions and antibiotics exerted an exceptional effective contact-killing effect on the bacteria. Alginate aerogel, as the third sustaining-release layer, delayed the release of drugs and copper ions, and absorbed excess tissue fluid as well.

In this study, a new composite antibacterial aerogel for orthopedic surgery was designed and prepared, which was composed of copper‑carbon dots complex binding with tigecycline (Cu/TGC@PDA) and alginate aerogel, the fabrication process was shown as Scheme 1 . Moreover, the antibacterial performance of the alginate aerogel was investigated. In vitro experiment showed that the Cu/TGC@PDA alginate aerogel had an exceptional effective effect on antibacterial activity and maintained 18 days on the effective antibacterial concentration. In addition, the Cu/TGC@PDA alginate aerogel presented significant antibacterial activity and negligible biotoxicity (average cell viability >75%). These results confirmed that Cu/TGC@PDA alginate aerogel was suitable for the treatment of bone tissue infection.

Chitosan (deacetylation >95%) was purchased from Sanland Chemical Co. LTD. Citric acid (CA analytical grade), acetic acid (analytical grade), cupric chloride dihydrate (analytical grade), hydrazine hydrate (80%), and anhydrous ethanol (analytical grade) were purchased from TianJing DaMao Reagent Corporation. Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co. LTD. Sodium alginate (SA ŋ = 200 ± 20 mPa.s) and dopamine hydrochloride were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Sodium periodate was obtained from Shandong XiYa Chemical industry Co. LTD. Gelatin (Gel) was provided by Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co. LTD. Tigecycline (TGC) for injection was obtained from Patheon Italia S.p.A.

Cu crystal was prepared as well as our previously reported work with modest modifications. In brief, 0.2 g chitosan was dissolved in 130 mL of 1% acetic acid solution, followed by the addition of 0.52 g citric acid. The mixture was stirred in a flask for 10 min, then was transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature, finally a yellow CDs solution was obtained.

20 mL 0.1 M CuCl2 was mixed with 20 mL obtained CDs solution, and then adding 40 mL ultrapure water, making the final volume of mixtures to be 80 mL. 80 g NaOH was dissolved in the solution with agitation, followed by 6.4 mL 35% hydrazine hydrate with agitation at 70 °C for 16 h. And then, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min (HC-3518, ZONKIA, China) for 15 min, followed by wish with ultrapure water and ethyl alcohol for six times. The precipitates were lyophilized at −80 °C to obtain the Cu crystal.

TGC was dissolved in 20 mL Tris solution, followed by added 20 mg Cu crystal in a dark condition with stirring for 16 h. Nest, the TGC mixture was added into polymerized dopamine solution (2 mg/mL) with stirring overnight. And a brow Cu/TGC@PDA solution was obtained then.

Oxidation sodium alginate (OSA) was prepared by adding sodium alginate (5 g) and sodium periodate (4.6 g) to water at room temperature with stirring for 6 h. The solution was added excessive ethyl alcohol to remove the spent oxidant and then freeze-dried, thus oxidation sodium alginate was obtained.

2% (w/w) OSA solution and 4% (w/w) GT solution were equal volume mixed with agitation for 1 h, making the final volume of mixture to be 30 mL, and then 3 mL Cu/TGC@PDA solution was added. After another 10 min, the Cu/TGC@PDA composite solution was flowed in a culture dish to freeze at −20 °C for 20 h, then lyophilized for 24 h to obtain the Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Cu crystal and aerogel were carried on Nicolet 6700 Infrared Detector (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The surface morphologies of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel and Cu crystal were characterized by thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, FEI Quanta-400F). The crystalline structure of Cu crystal was analyzed by a X-ray diffraction analyzer (XRD, Rigaku D/max-IIIA, USA) with Cu Ka radiation as the exciting source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of drug-loading and entrapment efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The drug-loading capacity and drug entrapment efficiency of Cu/TGC were investigated by UV–Vis spectra. According to the weight ratio (TGC:Cu): 1:5, 1:10, 1:25 and 1:50, 5 mg of TGC was mixed with the Cu, followed by the addition of 20 mL tris solution and stirring of 16 h in a dark condition. And then, the mixture was separated by a centrifuge at 10000 rpm/min for 30 min and analyzed by spectrophotometer.

The drug-loading and entrapment efficiency was calculated by the following equations:
Drug loading (DL):
DL =
M F
−
C F
V
/
M C
∗ 100 %
Entrapment efficiency(EE):
EE =
M F
−
C F
V
M F
× 100 %
where MF is the initial weight of tigecycline, CF is the concentration of tigecycline, V is the volume of absolute ethyl alcohol, MC is the weight of Cu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of swelling properties and retention properties
ce:section.ce:para:
Swelling studies for the aerogel were conducted by using the gravimetric method. After weighting (Wb), the aerogel were dried in the oven at 37 °C for 24 h; then, the aerogel were suspended in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution at 37 °C for 2 days to equilibrate and harvested to be weighted (Wa) at different times. The same sample was tested three times. The swelling ratio was determined using the following equation:
swelling ratio
%
=
W a
−
W b
/
W b
× 100 % .

E. coli strain DH5α was grown in Luria−Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C. After the appearance of many colonies, they were then transferred to a fresh medium and the final bacterial concentration was standardized to 105 CFU/mL. The antibacterial activity of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel was evaluated using the disk diffusion method. Circular specimens (diameter = 6 mm) of the aerogel were placed in different places of one inoculated Petri dish. After 24 h of incubation, the inhibition zone of the aerogel was measured with a Vernier caliper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release studies and ion release measurements
ce:section.ce:para: To obtained the stability and release rate of TGC from Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel, composite aerogel was placed in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 2000 Da). The dialysis bag was then kept in 100 mL of PBS (PH = 6.8)in an conical flask and gently shaken at 37 °C. At predetermined time intervals, 1 mL of the solution was taken out to test the released amount of TGC by HPLC at 244 nm. The released concentration of copper ions from the aerogel was assessed by Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (ASS). To maintain a constant volume, 1 mL fresh PBS was added after each sampling.

Mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in an incubator with 5% CO2. The cytotoxicity activities of the composite aerogel against L929 cells were using CCK-8 assay. The cells were plated in the 96-well plate at 5000 cells/well and incubated for 24 h before the test, and then the medium was replaced with 200 μL of serum free medium. The composite aerogel was incubated with the remaining cells for the next 24 h. To each well, 20 μL of CCK-8 solution was added. Following incubated for an additional 4 h at 37 °C, the absorbance of the cell suspension was recorded at 450 nm.

The human umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells (hUC-MSCs) (Guangzhou Institute of Biological Medicine and Health) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum, 100 U/mL penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin. To measure the cell viability of hUC-MSCs cultured the aerogel, live/dead viability/cytotoxicity test was carried out. After 1, 3 and 5 days of cultivation, cell-aerogel complex was taken out from medium and washed with PBS for three times. Then, the cell-aerogel complex was stained with fresh PBS solution contains 2 μM calcein-AM and 1 μM ethidium homodimer (EthD-1) for 30 min at 37 °C. Finally, fluorescence microscope (Olympus IX71, Tokyo, Japan) was selected to observe the live/dead cell morphology.

The proliferation of hUC-MSCs on aerogel was quantitatively analyzed by CCK-8 assay. In brief, 10 μL CCK-8 kit was added into the culture media after 1, 3 and 5 days of incubation, the incubation lasted for 30 min at 37 °C. Thereafter, the absorbances of test solutions were measured using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay reader (Bio-Tex) at 450 nm.

In this study, Cu/TGC@PDA complex was successfully synthesized via a two-step synthetic route. As Scheme 1 shows, the synthesis was achieved in a basic aqueous solution by using CDs as a grown additive, copper chloride (CuCl2) as a copper precursor, hydrazine hydrate (N2H2) as a reducing agent for Cu2+, and dopamine (DA) as a slow-release layer [45]. The aerogel serves as the Cu@TGC/PDA complex carrier, and it also provides a slow-release effect for TGC. The Tigecycline was loaded on Cu crystal via the electrostatic interaction between the amido groups in the molecule of Tigecycline and Cu crystal. To investigate the structures transformation of Cu particles under the guidance of CDs, the typical SEM image of the different stage of Cu was observed, as shown in Figs. 1 and S1. According to Fig. 1a, the Cu particles exhibited an irregular structure and some “tentacles” had been found on the surface. After being surface-modified with CDs in the initial reaction period, a rough carbon layer on the Cu crystal was observed in Fig. S1. At the later reaction stage, Fig. 1b shows the existence of Cu crystal with a size range from 500 nm to 1 μm, and reveals that the “tentacles” disappear from the surface of Cu crystal, this indicated that CDs induced the self-assembly of Cu crystal. From Fig. 1c, it can be observed that the wrapping PDA around the complex did not affect the morphology of Cu crystal. Fig. S2 shows a kind of octahedral Cu crystal. The partially amplifying images displayed that the facet of Cu octahedron crystal was rough, and this morphology was formed by ordered aggregation of nanoparticles (Fig. S2 top). Based on the SEM images, the particle size of Cu and Cu/TGC is measured to be 543.9 ± 0.87 nm and 552.7 ± 0.42 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d). The hydrodynamic diameters of Cu/TGC@PDA barely slightly increased to 584.2 ± 0.23 nm after PDA loading, due to the low molecular weight of PDA and the thin coating layer. The EDX spectrum of Cu/TGC displayed peaks associated with four main elements (carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and copper) that constituted Cu (Figs. S3 and S4). And the content of carbon and oxygen is 40.03% and 16.41%, which are higher than nitrogen (3.17%).

Zeta potential of Cu, TGC, Cu/TGC, and Cu/TGC@PDA were detected and shown in Table 1 . It was clearly observed that the Cu crystal was highly negatively charged (−14.23 ± 0.2 mV), which was attributed to the ionization of citric acid groups loaded on CDs in aqueous solution. The average Zeta potential of TGC was 10.11 ± 0.6 mV, while the Zeta potential of Cu/TGC reduced to −3.69 ± 0.5 mV, demonstrating that the TGC was successfully loaded on Cu. The Tigecycline was loaded on Cu crystal through the electrostatic interaction. Remarkably, the Zeta potential of Cu/TGC@PDA was 10.13 ± 2.3 mV which was higher than that of both TGC and Cu/TGC. Therefore, the surface charge of Cu/TGC@PDA was positive following by PDA loaded. The results of FT-IR spectra were shown in Fig. 1e. The FT-IR absorption characteristic of chitosan and citric acid in the spectra of Cu crystal, including the vibration absorption of OH and NH at 3405 cm−1, methyl and methylene CH occupying the region of 2920 cm−1 and 2877 cm−1, the stretch of the carboxyl bond CO at 1725 cm−1 and the in-plane bending vibration of NH and OH at 1607 cm−1 and 1368 cm−1. Interesting, the band at 730 cm−1 exhibited the characteristic adsorption peaks of Cu crystal. The FT-IR spectra of the TGC exhibited the characteristic adsorption peaks at 1642 cm−1. The IR spectrum of Cu/TGC complex indicated the successful coordination between Cu and TGC via amine and hydroxyl functional groups. In detail, the bands on 3424 cm−1 showed a stronger peak, which could be attributed to the presence of the OH stretching vibration absorption peak overlaps with the NH stretching vibration absorption peak. And the characteristic adsorption peaks of TGC also appeared in Cu/TGC, this indicated that TGC was successfully loaded on Cu. Moreover, the Penta-substituted benzene of -C=H at 870 cm−1. The FT-IR spectra of the Cu/TGC@PDA complex exhibited most of the characteristic absorption peaks of Cu/TGC, with some differences. The bond at the range of 3400–3000 cm−1 and 1600–1300 cm−1 showed a stronger peak, indicating polydopamine increased the hydroxyl and amino groups. The evidence of TGC loading on Cu was shown in the EDS as well (Figs. S2 and S3). XRD analyses (Fig. 1f) identified that three sharp peaks of Cu emerged, which coincided with three main peaks of face-centered cubic copper (JCPDS card no.04-0836), representing the (111) plane, (200) plane, (220) plane and (311) plane of pure copper crystals, respectively.

To confirm the successful synthesis of complex aerogel, FT-IR spectroscopy was performed. As shown in Fig. 2c, in the IR spectroscopy of oxide sodium alginate (OSA), a new peak at 1728 cm−1 appeared, which was the CO vibration absorption peak of the aldehyde group. And another characteristic adsorption peaks of the aldehyde group CH at 2850 cm−1. The result of OSA indicated that aldehyde sodium alginate was successfully prepared. Comparing with OSA and complex aerogel, the disappearance of the band at 1728 cm−1 indicated the involvement of the aldehyde group in the complex aerogel.

The surface of aerogel was observed by SEM technique. As shown in Fig. 2a, the aerogel exhibited a loose porous network structure. After the blending of Cu/TGC@PDA, the aerogel was smooth, but some small granular particles appeared on the surface as presented in Fig. 2b. The most noticeable change was that the blending of Cu/TGC@PDA altered the material distribution and the surface topography markedly.

As a soft tissue antibacterial dressing, the composite aerogel should has good hygroscopy activity. The hygroscopy properties of aerogel, Cu aerogel, Cu/TGC aerogel and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel were tested their swelling rate by immersing them in PBS (pH 7.4) at 37 °C for 24 h, the results were presented in Fig. 3b. The curve of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel shows 4769% rate of water absorption, and the others show 4349%, 4290% and 4527%. All the complex aerogel equilibrium within 4 h and were changed into spongy composites (Fig. 3a). Specifically, compared with the control group, the water absorption rate of Cu and Cu/TGC aerogel decreased, likely due to the blending of high density particles, which could increase the density of the aerogel and reduced swelling rate. Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel showed the best hygroscopic property of these composites, possibly because of the effect of dopamine. This result was related to that dopamine possesses a great many hydrophilic groups, which increased the rate of water absorption for aerogel. Thus, Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel has good water absorption property, which could maintain the moisture content of the wound and avoid dehydration.

In order to confirm the optimal amount of drug loaded on the Cu crystal, we changed the initial mass ratio of the Cu/TGC ranged from 1:5 to 1:50 as depicted in Table 2 . The results showed that a higher mass ratio of Cu/TGC led to greater entrapment efficiency. Interestingly, the drug loading rate had a tendency of first go up and then go down, and the maximal drug loading sat at the mass ratio of 1:10. The reason was that the combine of Cu/TGC was mainly attributed to the existence of opposite charges between Cu crystal and TGC. The loading of TGC reached its saturation in the neighborhood of the mass ratio of 1:10, hereafter the increase of mass ratio had litter effect on drug loading amount. The 1:20 Cu/TGC sample had a drug loading efficiency and embedding ratio of approximately 7.84% and 70.34%. Therefore, the mass ratio of 1:20 was selected as the optimal ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release from Cu/TGC@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: Tigecycline (TGC) is a glyceryl tetracycline active antibiotic, with a broad range of activity against bacteria that has a rich hydroxyl group on the surface and can be loaded on the surface of Cu through electrostatic interactions. FT-IR spectroscopy and Zeta potential showed that TGC was successfully loaded on the Cu crystal. The in vitro release behaviors of TGC from TGC aerogel, Cu/TGC aerogel and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel were tested in PBS (pH = 7.4) at 37 °C by HPLC at 247 nm (Fig. 4a). The release profiles evidenced a quick release during the first 10 h, reaching a 16.6% of the loaded TGC amount of TGC aerogel, and the other aerogel showed smooth release curve. Because of the surface of aerogel was loose and porous and the pore diameter is larger, which is conducive to the accelerated release of drugs. In the next stage, the following release of TGC aerogel showed a decline trend after 120 h, likely due to the oxidation degradation of TGC. However, in Cu/TGC and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel, TGC contains functional groups of electron donors, which could be used as organic ligands to complex with copper ions. Some reports indicated that interactions between TGC and heavy metals can affect the release behavior [46]. Thus, in the following stage, prolonged release profiles over 180 h are observed, with a cumulative percentage of 35% for Cu/TGC aerogel and 27% for Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel. In summary, Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel could be used as a good carrier of TGC, with a good prospect for clinical application.

The in vitro release profiles of Cu2+ from Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel were obtained via the atomic absorption spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4b, Cu aerogel shown a quick release within 2 days, reaching 36.6% of the total copper content. After 3 days, the release rate of copper ions became slow and maintained in 33% of the total copper content, perhaps because of the adsorption of copper by aerogel. Owing to the metal chelation of Sodium alginate and controlled nanosized pore structure, the aerogel could adsorb copper ions, which caused that Cu aerogel went into a slow and sustainable Cu2+ release after 3 days. PDA layer possessed reducing and stabilizing abilities for the metal nanoparticles, which could control the released of metal ions [47]. It was found that the concentration of copper ions released from the Cu@PDA aerogel moderately increase during the first 7 days, and remained stable over the subsequent 10 days. The total copper release of the Cu@PDA aerogel was 49.1% of total copper content, which was higher than that of Cu aerogel. These results indicated that the surface modification with PDA made contribution to the sustainable release of Cu from Cu@PDA aerogel. In contrast, the whole release process of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel could be divided into four stages. The first step showed a quick release as Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel release 29.6% of total copper content within 2 days. In the following 3–7 days, the copper ions showed slow release over time, which mainly due to dopamine self-polymerize on the surface of copper and the absorption of alginate. The release curve illuminated a quick release during 8–11 days, which was likely ascribed to the oxidative decomposition of dopamine. After a prolonged release stage over 12 days, a cumulative percentage of 80% Cu was released for the total copper content. These results indicated that Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel has advantages on the slow releasing of Cu2+.

To investigate the effect of the aerogel on the proliferation of normal cells, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of our composites on a mammalian standard cell line, L929 mouse fibroblast cells, using the CCK-8 assay. The solutions of TGC, Cu, PDA, Cu/TGC and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel were incubated with mouse fibroblast cells for 24 h, respectively. The control group was SA-Gel aerogel. As shown in Fig. 5 , the viability values of cells with Cu, PDA and Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel were higher than 70% under 2 mg/mL concentration. And the cytotoxicity of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel gradually increased with the increase of concentration, which due to the drug toxicity and the sudden released of copper ions resulting copper toxicity [48]. Despite the fact that a high dose of Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel presented observable cytotoxicity (8 mg/mL), which was reasonable and acceptable compared with previous studies [49].

Taking both the cytotoxicity and cell viability into considering, 4 mg/mL Cu/TGC@PDA was selected for further biological research. The SA-Gel aerogel were used as control group. Cell viability of hUC-MSCs on the Cu/TGC@PDA and SA-Gel aerogel was shown in Fig. 6a. The Cu/TGC@PDA and SA-Gel aerogel showed good cytocompatibility and were able to support cell to attach. In order to investigate the proliferation capacity of hUC-MSCs on Cu/TGC@PDA and SA-Gel aerogel, CCK-8 assay was performed (Fig. 6b). Clearly, the cells on both aerogel continue to multiply, indicating the aerogel both promoted cell proliferation. In comparison with the SA-Gel aerogel, an appreciably higher proliferation capacity of cells was obtained on Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel in 5 days. The hydrophilic groups of polydopamine (such as OH−and NH2+) could regulate the surface charge of the aerogel, which promotes cell adhesion and stretching [50]. Thus, the antibacterial activity and cytocompatibility experiments indicated that the aerogel exhibited good cytocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antimicrobial activity of Cu/TGC@PDA for bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the antimicrobial of TGC, Cu, Cu/TGC and Cu/TGC@PDA, E. coli and S. aureus were used as model bacteria. The results were shown in Fig. 7a and b, all samples showed good response against bacteria. The biggest inhibition zone was measured in the Cu/TGC@PDA, with average outside and inside diameter against E. coli of 30.1 mm and 21 mm, respectively. It was noteworthy that the antibacterial activity of Cu/TGC@PDA was relatively weaker than Cu/TGC against S. aureus, which may be ascribed to the PDA layer which would interfere the release behavior of TGC, in this context, TGC played an important role in inhibiting the growth of S. aureus [51]. In addition, the E. coli depicted stronger sensitivity to copper nanoparticles than S. aureus [52]. Therefore, combining with Cu and TGC, the resultant aerogel exhibited an enhanced antibacterial activity against both E. coli and S. aureus rather than any single strategy. The image of bacteriostatic tablet (Fig. 7c) demonstrated that Cu/TGC@PDA had excellent antibacterial activity.

It was reported that the minimum inhibitory concentration of Cu2+ was 10−6 M, and showed nontoxic to L929 cells at the concentration of 10−4 M [53]. In order to determine the optimal dose of Cu/TGC@PDA in the aerogel, the bacterial cultures containing antibacterial nanoparticles cultured for different concentration on LB agar plates, and then cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. And we chose 6.4 mg/mL cupric chloride solution as a positive control. As Fig. 7d showed, the antimicrobial activity of 2 mg/mL Cu/TGC@PDA was better than the positive control. And it was clear that the antibacterial activity between different concentration had small differences, but showing an obvious cytotoxicity with high concentrations (>6 mg/mL) (Fig. 5). These results showed that the concentration of less than 6 mg/mL would be a comprehensive consideration.

In summary, an alginate aerogel (Cu/TGC@PDA) with exceptional effective antibacterial based on the combinatorial treatments of TGC and Cu crystal were prepared. Cu crystal acted as a copper source and reservoir for loading drugs, which endowed the aerogel with a capability of sustained releasing copper ions and antibiotics. Polydopamine was introduced as slow-release layer, effectively prolong the release of tigecycline. In vitro antimicrobial experiments indicated that Cu/TGC@PDA alginate aerogel exhibited higher antibacterial ability than both the single TGC and Cu crystal. In addition, Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel also exhibited rich pores, good swelling and could be removed easily. Therefore, taking account into its long-term antibacterial activity and excellent biocompatibility, Cu/TGC@PDA aerogel was suitable for the application of bone tissue infectious.
For transparency, we outlined all authors' individual contributions to the paper as follow:
Xia-Xiao Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Original Draft.
Yu Zhang: Data curation, Validation, Formal analysis, Project administration.
Tian Hu: Writing-Review & Editing.
Wei-Xiong Li: Conceptualization.
Zeng-Lin Li: Visualization.
Han-Jian Hu: Validation.
Shui-Rong Zhu: Methodology, Validation.
Wen-Zhao Chen: Validation.
Chu-Song Zhou: Resources, Writing-Review & Editing, Supervision.
Gang-Biao Jiang: Resources, Writing-Review & Editing, Supervision, Data Curation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thank for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China [41671302, 41977127], the Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou City [201607010183] and the Open Research Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology [2018KF05].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.11.075.
